title: Reading a Statement & Opening Your First Account
summary: What's actually on that page of numbers, and how teens open accounts at all.
doThisToday: If you have any account already, open the app and find one transaction you don't recognize or remember — then figure out what it was.

cards.prompt: A bank statement lists every transaction in your account over a period: the date, what it was, how much, and your running balance after each one.
cards.explanation: Reading one takes two minutes and tells you exactly where your money went — no guessing required.

cards.prompt: On your statement, you see: "07/03 — POS Withdrawal — Corner Market — $4.50 — Balance: $85.20." What does this line tell you?
options: You spent $4.50 at Corner Market on July 3rd, leaving $85.20 in the account afterward
cards.explanation: "POS Withdrawal" just means a card purchase (point of sale). Every line on a statement follows this same pattern: what happened, how much, and your balance right after.

cards.prompt: Scrolling your statement, you spot a $9.99 charge labeled a subscription you don't remember signing up for. What should you do?
options: Look it up, and if it's unrecognized or unwanted, cancel it and consider disputing the charge with your bank
Close your entire bank account immediately
cards.explanation: This is exactly why checking statements regularly matters — forgotten subscriptions and unauthorized charges hide in plain sight if nobody's looking.

cards.prompt: Bank statements are full of abbreviations that look confusing but aren't.
cards.frontText: What do the common abbreviations POS, ACH, and ATM WD mean on a bank statement?
cards.backText: POS = Point of Sale (a card purchase). ACH = Automated Clearing House (a bank transfer, like direct deposit or a bill autopay). ATM WD = ATM Withdrawal (cash you took out). Once you know these three, most of a statement makes sense at a glance.
cards.explanation: None of it is secret code — it's just shorthand for a handful of common transaction types.

cards.prompt: True or false: you have to be 18 to open any kind of bank account.
cards.explanation: Many banks offer teen or custodial accounts for minors, usually co-owned or supervised by a parent until you turn 18. Some start as young as 13, sometimes younger with a parent as primary owner.

cards.prompt: A custodial or teen account usually needs a parent or guardian as a co-owner or supervisor.
cards.explanation: They can often see your transactions and may need to approve certain actions — but the account, debit card, and habit-building are yours.

cards.prompt: What should you look for when choosing your first bank account?
options: The bank with the flashiest ads
No monthly fees, no minimum balance requirement, and an app you'll actually use to check things regularly
Whichever bank has the most branch locations, even if you'll never visit one
cards.explanation: Most teens do all their banking from a phone. A clean app and zero fees for having a normal balance matter far more than physical branches or flashy marketing.

cards.prompt: Opening your first account isn't complicated — bring ID, expect a parent to be involved, and pick a bank with no fees and a solid app.
cards.explanation: The account itself is just a tool. Everything you've learned this unit — checking vs. savings, avoiding fees, reading statements — is what makes that tool actually work for you.
